did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid AI architecture named MultiExCam for skin lesion classification (melanoma detection). The method integrates deep learning (Convolutional Neural Network/ResNet50 for feature extraction and classification) and machine learning (four additional ML models and a Feed Forward Neural Network ensemble with gating and attention). It also employs Explainable AI techniques (GradCAM and SHAP) for interpretability. Results were obtained using datasets HAM10000, ISIC, and MED-NODE.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper introduces a novel architecture named MultiExCam for skin lesion classification, detailing specific methods (hybrid deep learning and machine learning, GradCAM, SHAP), experimental validation on three datasets (HAM10000, ISIC, MED-NODE), and original results including AUC and F1-scores. It includes sections for Methods, Results, and Conclusion, indicating it is an original experimental study rather than a review.

title: MultiExCam: A multi approach and explainable artificial intelligence architecture for skin lesion classification
journal: Computer Methods and Programs in Biomedicine
brief_summary: This study introduces MultiExCam, a novel hybrid artificial intelligence architecture for skin lesion classification that integrates deep learning feature extraction with machine learning models to detect cutaneous melanoma. The method utilizes a Convolutional Neural Network for deep feature extraction, which is combined with handcrafted statistical features to train four machine learning models, subsequently fused via a Feed Forward Neural Network with gating and attention mechanisms. The architecture was validated on three diverse datasets: HAM10000, ISIC, and MED-NODE, employing a binary classification task for melanoma versus common nevi. MultiExCam achieved high diagnostic performance with AUC scores of 97%, 91%, and 98%, and F1-scores of 92%, 87%, and 94% across the respective datasets. The system incorporates Explainable AI techniques, specifically GradCAM and SHAP, to provide clinically meaningful visualizations and feature importance that align with established dermatological criteria. The results demonstrate that this true hybrid integration outperforms baseline deep learning models while offering superior clinical interpretability for decision support.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary malignant class studied for detection. Included in HAM10000 (1,134 images), ISIC (454 images), and MED-NODE datasets. The paper focuses on binary classification of melanoma versus common nevi.

Primary benign class used as the negative control in the binary classification task. Included in HAM10000 (6,705 images, downsampled to 1,134 for balance), ISIC (373 images), and MED-NODE datasets. The study specifically addresses the 'Melanoma/Nevus' binary classification task.

Mentioned in the Introduction as a type of lesion that presents characteristics associated with malignant lesions but has a benign nature, and is studied by domain experts due to the likelihood of developing melanoma. Not explicitly listed as a separate experimental class in the results.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma (positive class) versus common nevi (negative class).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'binary classification' for 'melanoma detection'. The model outputs a scalar probability via a sigmoid activation function representing the probability of the positive class (melanoma). The final prediction is a discrete class label derived from this probability. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, F1-Score, AUC, Kappa, and MCC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A dataset released as part of the ISIC 2018 Challenge, comprising 10,000 RGB dermoscopic images (600x450 pixels) sourced from diverse populations. For this study, a balanced subset was created by downsampling the majority class (common nevus) to match the minority class (melanoma).

use.training: Official training and validation sets were merged, shuffled, and partitioned into new training (70%), validation (15%), and test (15%) sets. Data augmentation was applied to the training set.
use.validation: Used for model validation during the 70-15-15 split.

datasets_used.name: ISIC (Skin Cancer Detection Dataset)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset on Kaggle derived from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2019 and 2020 competitions. It contains high-resolution RGB images with complex and ambiguous cases. For this study, only images labeled as melanoma and nevus were retained.

use.training: Used for training the MultiExCam architecture.

datasets_used.name: MED-NODE
datasets_used.description: A dataset developed by the Department of Dermatology at the University Medical Center Groningen. It is publicly available and used to validate the generalizability of the proposed framework.

use.training: Used for training the MultiExCam architecture.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the performance of machine learning models and compute statistical metrics (mean, standard deviation, min, max) across different data folds.
To estimate the uncertainty of the mean performance metrics (Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, NPV, F1-Score, Kappa, MCC) derived from the 5-fold cross-validation.
To perform hyperparameter optimization for the machine learning models, utilizing 5-fold cross-validation for validation during the search.
To quantify the variability or dispersion of performance metrics across the cross-validation folds.
To measure the difference between the predicted values and the actual values of the performance metrics across the cross-validation folds.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals and standard deviations derived from 5-fold cross-validation to estimate uncertainty and stability, but no statistical hypothesis tests comparing groups were performed.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: 256 deep features extracted from the dense layer of the customized pre-trained ResNet50 network
architecture_details.hand_crafted_features: 11 features encoding color (moments, dominant hue) and texture (GLCM statistics)
architecture_details.ensemble_input_vector: 272-dimensional vector (256 deep features + 11 hand-crafted features + 5 model prediction scores)
architecture_details.ensemble_model: Feed Forward Neural Network (FFNN) with gating mechanism (residual connections) and attention mechanism
architecture_details.ensemble_branches: Multi-branch architecture handling deep features, hand-crafted features, and prediction scores separately
architecture_details.ml_model_selection: SVM, RF, KNN, GNB for HAM10000; SVM, RF, KNN, GB for ISIC; SVM, RF, GNB, XGB for MED-NODE

classification_or_output_head.type: Custom classification head for ResNet50; Final FFNN output

preprocessing_techniques: Two-stage preprocessing pipeline for artifact removal and standardization

Step I: Body hair removal using morphological Black Hat operation (17x17 structuring element) on grayscale images, thresholding to create binary mask, and inpainting algorithm to reconstruct underlying content
Step II: Dark border correction via threshold-based analysis of peripheral pixel intensities to detect vignetting artifacts
Corrective transformation for dark borders: mild zoom-in (scaling factor 1.3) followed by resizing via interpolation to 224x224 pixels
Manual region-of-interest (ROI) selection to isolate lesion areas and remove irrelevant elements (jewelry, background, clothing)
Data augmentation on training set including rotation, flipping, and zooming
Data balancing via down-sampling of the majority class (common nevus) to match the minority class (melanoma) for the HAM10000 dataset

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training set using the ImageDataGenerator utility from the Keras library to enhance model generalization and reduce overfitting. Specific angles or probabilities were not reported.
Applied to the training set using the ImageDataGenerator utility from the Keras library. Specific axis or probabilities were not reported.
Applied to the training set using the ImageDataGenerator utility from the Keras library. Specific zoom ranges or probabilities were not reported.
Used for class balancing on the HAM10000 dataset by down-sampling the majority class (common nevus) to match the minority class (melanoma), resulting in 1,134 images per class.

Evaluated as a class balancing strategy in ablation studies (Table 7, Config 2 and 4) but resulted in dropped performance compared to down-sampling. Specific implementation details (e.g., SMOTE) were not provided, only that it led to an overabundance of synthetic examples.

conclusions: MultiExCam establishes a new paradigm for AI-assisted dermatological diagnosis by demonstrating that true hybrid integration of deep learning and machine learning, combined with comprehensive explainability techniques, can achieve both superior diagnostic performance and clinical interpretability.

The MultiExCam architecture achieved robust performance across three diverse datasets (HAM10000, ISIC, MED-NODE), with AUC scores of 97%, 91%, and 98% respectively, and F1-scores of 92%, 87%, and 94%.

Comprehensive ablation studies validated the importance of the preprocessing pipeline and ensemble integration, showing that the hybrid approach consistently outperformed baseline deep learning models by 1–3 percentage points.

Unlike existing compartmentalized hybrid solutions, MultiExCam’s adaptive ensemble architecture learns personalized decision strategies for individual lesions, mimicking expert dermatological workflows that integrate multiple evidence sources.

Explainability analysis using GradCAM and SHAP revealed clinically meaningful activation patterns corresponding to established diagnostic criteria, including asymmetry, border irregularity, and color variation.

The architecture’s ability to provide accurate classifications while explaining prediction rationale addresses critical requirements for medical AI adoption, offering a promising foundation for clinical decision support systems in melanoma detection.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single deep learning backbone (ResNet50), which may miss complementary patterns that multi-backbone or hybrid CNN-transformer ensembles could capture.
The hand-crafted feature set is limited to color and texture, lacking geometric and clinically validated dermatological indices.
The model lacks segmentation, which limits precise lesion boundary modeling and may hinder performance on complex lesion types like dysplastic nevi.
Validation was performed on three public datasets (HAM10000, ISIC, MED-NODE) but lacks external validation across diverse acquisition devices, lighting conditions, and patient demographics.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing (p-values); uncertainty is estimated via 95% confidence intervals from 5-fold cross-validation.
Performance on ambiguous or low-contrast lesions remains challenging, with GradCAM occasionally failing to highlight regions due to diffuse pigmentation.
Computational overhead from the ensemble architecture may hinder deployment in constrained environments; model compression and lightweight design are noted as future needs.
The study addresses only binary classification (melanoma vs. common nevus), excluding other skin cancer types or multi-class scenarios.
